Frame generation increases displayed FPS
but does not double your real render throughput.
Latency depends mostly on base FPS.
Example:60 real FPS + frame gen → 120 displayed FPS
Input latency still behaves closer to 60 FPS
That’s why:
Competitive multiplayer players avoid frame gen
It’s best for cinematic single-player 
So calling it “basically a single-player feature” is a reasonable opinion in competitive contexts.
That’s a valid technical stance.

Jensen Huang was right — “it would be impossible without artificial intelligence.” The GPU industry has hit a technological wall. Maintaining high frame rates with increasingly smaller and more efficient components is becoming economically unsustainable. The traditional path — raw performance scaling — no longer makes sense. NVIDIA fully understands this.

AMD still seems to miss the point. Upscaling is not the same as machine learning-based solutions like DLSS. Without AI support, FSR has clear limitations and will inevitably fall behind.

People are now accusing NVIDIA of deception — claiming it’s all just marketing, that the new 12VHPWR connectors are a ploy to force hardware upgrades. Maybe that’s true. Maybe it’s just strategy. But it doesn’t matter anymore.

The core truth is this: the future of image processing is driven by machine learning. There’s no turning back. Whether DLSS is marketing or not is irrelevant — it’s the only viable path forward.

These GPUs should no longer be considered a new generation, they are just "40-series Plus" GPUs. (5070 = 4070 Super Plus/5080 = 4080 Super Plus/5090 = 4090 Super) 

This is because they offer either identical performance to their 40 series counter parts, have performance uplift identical to their raw resource uplift (5090 has 30% more RAM/Power Draw/CUDA Cores and unsurprisingly exactly 30% more performance than the 4090). GDDR7 and the new AI features aren't enough to justify calling them 50 series cards, cause everything else is basically the same.

Nvidia has done this in the past where a new generation was made on the same architecture, but the last time they did this was in 2013 with the 600 series and I can't find information on how well/poorly that was received. Nvidia hasn't been doing this for over a decade and it should have stayed that way, people got used to performance uplifts with each new generation and the 50 series just doesn't do anything new.

These GPUs should never have been called the 50 series.

Reflex is a better tool if the base frame rate is also higher, but depending on the game, and particularly with newer games, the heavy demand on a 70 card like this or a 60 card in the future using multi frame generation as a crutch to achieve high frame rates from a low base frame rate will not be solved by reflex alone.

Games will also make this worse, with Monster Hunter Wilds starting a horrible precedent of using frame generation to define their requirements charts with no accounting for the negatives of interpolating so many frames in between the actual game engine frames.

And the biggest and most obvious elephant in the room, if these technologies alone absolutely yield a near enough experience, why are all those that are jacking up used 4090 prices buying those instead of a nice new 5070 with "4090 performance"?

​@ It's good. The only compatibility issues are with kernel level anti-cheat. 99.99% of games just work out of the box, and the games with normal (non-kernel) anti-cheat also just work (such as elden ring with easy anti-cheat). If you got Nvidia, you lose about 5% to 10% fps compared to windows, but if you got AMD, it's pretty much on par with windows if not better for some games.

For older games (from the windows 7 era and before), they actually work better on linux. On windows you have to install a bunch of GOG patches or community mods, when those games just run normally through WINE on Linux, without the patches.

All you have to do is install steam, and turn on proton compatibility for all games. Just makes sure you get a distro that is already pre-configured if you want it to "just work". If you install Arch for the memes, using your computer will become a full-time hobby

When the horrible performance of Dragons Dogma 2 and Monster Hunter Wilds forced me to use DLSS and Framegen to make it playable. I've become more accepting of these AI features. Especially now that I've tried DLSS 4 and FrameGen on my 3070 after a bit of tinkering. Although the flaws are still there, it allows me to play Dragon's Dogma 2 and Monster Hunter Wilds at respectable FPS (70-90), instead of sub 30fps with stuttering. FrameGen is a great way to get more longevity out of older cards! I still think adding anything more then 1 additional fake frame is bullshit and I cannot see how this makes things any better, but a single frame to "double" the fps is a remarkably great thing! Well, for games that doesn't require low response times or are of a competitive type. For Action-RPG or games less dependent on low-response times (i.e Souls games, Monster Hunter Wilds, Dragons dogma etc), FrameGen and DLSS is just phenomenal! 

But I would NOT recommend anyone buy a card for these features alone. I'm just saying that for most games and people, the flaws of these technologies aren't going to matter when you're actually playing. I'm for sure not noticing it when I'm playing but I can see the flaws if I take time to examine and look for it..

The kind of DLSS does not change the FPS you get signifcantly, mostly image quality

DLSS is nothing else but reducing the resolution and then upscaling it back up with AI. As an example, DLSS balanced will only render 60% of your selected resolution. 

That is what saves FPS: That you only render 60% of the pixels. If you use a 60% render scale or DLSS quality, FPS is nearly identical (bit better with render scale, not much). If you are using DLSS 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 or 4.0, does not matter, FPS are mostly the same, it is the image quality that changes

Therefore even DLSS 5.0 won't change anything regarding FPS

These GPUs should no longer be considered a new generation, they are just "40-series Plus" GPUs. (5070 = 4070 Super Plus/5080 = 4080 Super Plus/5090 = 4090 Super) 

This is because they offer either identical performance to their 40 series counter parts, have performance uplift identical to their raw resource uplift (5090 has 30% more RAM/Power Draw/CUDA Cores and unsurprisingly exactly 30% more performance than the 4090). GDDR7 and the new AI features aren't enough to justify calling them 50 series cards, cause everything else is basically the same.

Nvidia has done this in the past where a new generation was made on the same architecture, but the last time they did this was in 2013 with the 600 series and I can't find information on how well/poorly that was received. Nvidia hasn't been doing this for over a decade and it should have stayed that way, people got used to performance uplifts with each new generation and the 50 series just doesn't do anything new.

These GPUs should never have been called the 50 series.

at 1:45 the performance window is indicating that there is system ram being used for video.  Was this test done with the Nvidia Control Panel Setting "CUDA - Sysmem Fallback Policy" set to "Prefer No Sysmem Fallback" or "Driver Default" or "Prefer Sysmem Fallback"?  If it was set to "Prefer Sysmem Fallback" or "Driver Default" (both have the same effect in my experience with my card, a 3070ti 8gb), then it could be that if you set it to "Prefer No Sysmem Fallback" you might not encounter what looks like spillover into system ram for video, which in turn could cause a major change in performance.  My Speculation: the game doesn't know that that you have only 12gb of vram and "sees" more than that (vram plus some amount of system ram).  Nvidia introduced this "feature" around driver 532 if I recall correctly, but didn't give an option to disable it until several major versions later.   This was huge pain for those of us who also use our cards for local generative AI image software about 1-2 years ago, because when loading a large model the software wouldn't know that, for example, I "only" had 8gb of VRAM, so it in turn wouldn't initiate certain VRAM saving features (such as loading the model in fp16/bf16 instead of fp32), which would then cause a spillover as a 12gb AI model gets loaded into 8gb VRAM+System Ram memory (like shown at 1:45 in the video), which would then cause an enormous performance hit.  Those of us in the generative ai enthusiast community had to keep our drivers rolled back to 531.something until nvidia added the option to turn that "feature" off.

Saying that NVidia beats AMD at raytracing is a bit disingenuous, since the 40 series was the first series where ray tracing wasn't just a gimmick you pop on for a bit, get annoyed at the frame rate hit and turn back off.

The XTX was perfectly fine at this parlor trick, only the 4090 really brought the power to the table to make games playable with these tricks, and what NVidia doesn't want you to know is that it's not even good ray tracing in most of these games. The effect is barely better than the fake shadows and lighting we've played with for years for a performance hit of almost 60% and in many cases for every place ray tracing "fixed" a lighting mistake, it made a different lighting mistake.

There's a mod for Cyberpunk that adds "better" ray tracing to the game, that is more realistic, and can technically run on non RTX cards (as in the tricks they're using to achieve ray tracing quality can be done with or without their ray tracing cores). I'll care about ray tracing when it's either a standard feature (one where we aren't really bothering to separate it from rasterization performance), or the effect is actually as mindblowing as they're trying to say it is.

I'm a lighting guy, like literally went to school for lighting design, I actually love the idea of ray tracing, but losing over 60% of your frames for "fake tracing" is baffling to me.

Things just keep getting worse and worse for Nvidia.

They are ruining their reputation as a company who were once very good when it came to respecting their pc fanbase of customers that in turn showed confidence when it came to their stockholders. Many think that these prices were intentionally done to keep their stockholders satisfied, but when you really think about it, how the hell would want to risk purchasing Nvidia's stocks at this moment when the customers are unhappy with the results of this generation of graphics cards? Nvidia is shooting themselves in the foot here with a lackluster launch, and it's going to backfire on them significantly in the stock market. If I had shares of Nvidia, I don't see a better time to sell than right now. It's not just the price issue here that I'm talking about here. We are speaking about card cables melting, damaged motherboards, missing ROPs, and I wouldn't be surprised if we see these cards over-time fail much faster than any of Nvidia's prior cards. Something went seriously wrong during the production of the silicone wafers, and because these silicone wafer factories require 3 months to produce these chips, Nvidia just went ahead and sold some damaged wafers. Yes, silicone wafers take a minimum of 3 months to create just 1 processor due to the way these dyes need to be cured. Of course, these silicone chip manufacturers use massive wafer-shaped disks that can contain 81+ processors of different types to speed up production and to create processors like laptop chips as well. Every large circular wafer goes into dozens upon dozens of passes until each processor builds up to the thicknesses required and the design shaped. That's why they many talk about dyes when it comes to processors, because it uses multiple dyes to build up a processor. It spread a dye over a wafer, then goes into a hardening process, to a light sanding process, then goes into a cleaning process, then the entire wafer goes back to square one to build it's next layer. It goes through this process an absurd amount of times to build up these layers and is why these processors take so long to make, and is why a screwup of any kind during this precise manufacturing process can turn out to be catastrophic.

- What is also interesting is how each of the chips on the millions of circular wafers are classified. It's a kinda funny process actually. From your Intel i5 / i7 / i9's to Ultra processors, the Ryzen processors, to even Nvidia's 5070 / 5080 / 5090's of processors are labeled and priced based on the failure rates of each silicone chip made from the wafers. So, basically voltages are sent through each chip, that has dozens of processors made on these huge wafers are not the same. Every wafer is different from another wafer. When the performances are tested via voltages sent through each during the last stages of the manufacturing process, the most performant chip is the winner and is classified it's name. So, in terms of Nvidia's case, just to shorten up this explanation a bit, is that their most performant silicone chip is going to obviously be classified as the 5090, it then goes into a box, and shipped out. Every single processor fails, it just the degree of failure determines it's price during the test. Meaning, your that your 4060 / 4070/ 4070ti, 4080, were all developed the same way that could have been a 4090, but the chip failed, and the degree of failure is what drops it's classification down to the lower numbered cards that most pc gamers own today.

- In my personal opinion, and maybe it's bullshit, but I truly believe that the reason why we are seeing barely any stock of the 5090's currently was due to the amount of failed wafers during the manufacturing process, and when they sent voltages through these wafers, and cherry picking the good ones, they realized they screwed up. They were not expecting this significantly low number of failured cards when doing the failure rate testing. Maybe instead of a wafers that should have had at least 30x 5090's expected really only had 2x 5090's when testing the voltages, that are then thrown into a box, and ready to ship. This process is all automated too. I don't even think Nvidia expected this. That's where I'm at. The whole situation sucks. Someone screwed up, and it's going to be a bit until a corrected dye is made for pumping out the card Nvidia was targeting. Based on the supply right now, we saw tons of 5080's but not many 5090's, based on the failure rate, which is why there are no 5090's in stock. Hopefully Nvidia made a only a few bad batches, but due to the fact that there have been consistent issues with missing ROPs throughout this entire lineup of 50 Series graphics cards, tells me that there was without question a massive screwup of failed cards affected. I think the failure rate is just much higher than what Nvidia is claiming here, which is a shame.

Anyways, I'm sure most out there already know a bit about the manufacturing process, who may have more knowledge than me, but I just wanted to describe the process in as basic of terms as possible. These chip manufactures are highly restricted. Video cameras of anykind are not allowed into these facilities, and so those lucky enough to get a tour during the process can only describe what they see, but as technology improves, I'm sure these massive rooms are always changing.

I highly recommend checking out the latest videos on YouTube about how processors are made to get a better idea about this incredible manufacturing process.

Any card needs to have enough of a performance increase where we can turn on these settings without crippling our performance to even worse than they are now.

We deserved a better generation, maybe a refreshed 5080 with a further 25% perforomance increase will finally put it over the hill of what current and recent games require. But I'm not upgrading until I can get the sort of performance upgrade we used to expect. Generational improvements, not incremental.

I think the disappointment lacks context, though. For people wanting every generation to be a viable upgrade time, it's disappointing. But AMD is also disappointing this generation on anything outside of firesale prices on their only-midrange cards. Nvidia now has the podium with all three of the fastest GPUs in existence for consumers, which means they didn't have to push for a massive generational uplift.

And as a last thought, people who are first-time builders or who are sitting on rigs from 2016, they don't really care about generational uplift. They probably want a new model, widely compatible, do-everything GPU that plays games decently well at 1440p (or even 1080p in some people's case who don't care or only have a 1080p monitor) in the midrange price point. For them, they'll throw a 5070 in, hit their budget target, and play games with high settings at 1440p at very high framerates and be happy. You have to understand that THAT is the majority of the target market for this generation. NVidia refreshes to get a bit more performance, puts in compatibility for new AI upscaling to test it out (a la RTX20-series) and will throw the right hook next generation with the new fabrication process and new architectural changes.

Unexciting, but it fits the bill. $1500 for a full build with this card in it would make the casual gamer able to do whatever they want at 1440p with just leaving raytracing off.

@ "Devs can't optimize shit anymore"
That just isn't the Problem. Memory is cheap, there is no reason to overly optimize for it except for console compatability. The Problem here definitely is nvidia trying to force their customers to upgrade more frequently. 

As a comparison: 
in Q4 2010 the gtx 570 released with 1280MB of VRAM. In Q2 2016 the 1070 released with 8GB of VRAM (over sixfold (625%) increase over a timespan of  7 years). In Q1 2025 the rtx 5070 releases with 12 GB of VRAM (50% increase over a timespan of more than 8 years).

And this all in the context of the switch away from 1080 to 1440p(~80% more pixels) or 2160p(400% more pixels) and the addition of Raytracing which hogs VRAM and the 1070 hadn't implemented.

There is no real argument to be made, that this is a developers fault. They can't improve Visual quality and resolution and implement new lighting effects without requiring more memory capacity.

I think one of my favorite ways to think about memory is that it's like a highway and the cars are what is transfering the data from one point to another.  The memory capacity is how many lanes the highway has while the speed is how fast the cars can theoretically drive.  So, for example, if someone had a sports car, it would get to the other point faster than someone driving a eco car but the problem is traffic.  Regardless of how fast that sports car is, if there are too many cars on the road, you get congestion and the sports car can only travely as fast as the fastest car on the highway.  Having more lanes on the highway allows more throughput, meaning the roads are more easily able to handle the congestion by giving more lanes to use.
The same is with Memory.  Having faster speeds does nothing if there isn't enough throughput to get the data from one point to another.  Maybe someday, a company will come up with a solution to transfer some data faster than others, like an expressway on a highway does for traffic, but that isn't here today.
16 mb should be the minimum for mid range right now as we already see 12 isn't exactly enough memory to handle the throughput.  Nvidia is cutting corners and I think it may cost them in the end.

50-class 15-20% of the hardware of the 90-class 

60-class 25-35% of the hardware of the 90-class 

70-class roughly 50% of the hardware of the 90-class 

80-class 60-80% of the hardware of the 90-class

Let's get it simple. If you want to play games at console settings/resolutions, all you need is 10GB VRAM buffer. Consoles can only allocate 12,5GB for games and this volume is shared between CPU and GPU. So the GPU part can use <12,5GB. Thus a 10GB card can match consoles. Watch some comparisons, made by DF, if you do not want to believe me.
If we are talking about 12GB cards, they can run games at significantly higher settings/resolutions than consoles.
Thus until we see games, made specifically for PS6 with it's 24/32GB buffer, 12GB cards will be fine. PS6 is rumored to launch in late 2027. So we will only see games, that cross 10GB barrier in 2028, 3 years down the line.

3070 was released in 2020, the year PS5 was launched as well. No wonder that card had issues related to it's 8GB buffer. In 2021 some games have already been made for PS5 and required 10GB of VRAM to play at console settings.

Dar a entender que la memoria caché es un proceso aparte y que no tiene que ver con el consumo real del juego, es un error. Si vemos que la caché aumenta durante el juego es porque la GPU la necesita para acelerar accesos y cálculos; si la desactivas, el rendimiento se desploma. Eso demuestra que sí es necesaria. En conclusión: lo que vemos como consumo total de VRAM (texturas + buffers + caché) es lo que realmente importa. Por eso, siempre necesitamos más VRAM para que los juegos funcionen bien

En mi experiencia, los 12 GB de VRAM ya empiezan a quedarse cortos, al menos para ciertos usos. Con mi equipo actual (RTX 5080 Gaming Trio + Ryzen 7800X3D + 16 GB DDR5 a 6000 MHz), considero que los 16 GB de VRAM son fundamentales para jugar en 2K en Ultra con vistas de cambiar de monitor a 4k a medio plazo, sobre todo cuando mantengo en segundo plano música, vídeos u otros programas. De hecho, ya hay títulos que requieren esta cantidad de memoria, y aunque no son todos, la tendencia es clara: si inviertes en una gráfica, no lo haces solo pensando en el presente, sino también en los próximos 4 o 5 años.

Además, en mi caso uso unas Meta Quest 3 flamantes en VR y planeo exprimirlas al máximo jugando en Ultra, por lo que disponer de 16 GB de VRAM no es un lujo, sino una necesidad real. Lo mismo ocurre con aplicaciones de inteligencia artificial local (como LMStudio), donde esta memoria marca la diferencia sobretodo si las quieres entrenar para tener tu IA privada.

Lo que me cansa un poco es esa tendencia de algunos usuarios en foros, youtube y demás en a dar “lecciones de moralidad”  repitiendo que tener 16 GB es crear necesidades inexistentes y que caemos en el MARKETING o que directamente queremos “ir de sobrados”. Para mí ese argumento no tiene sentido: cada usuario tiene un uso personal y muy distinto de su hardware. En mi caso, juegos como Star Citizen, Resident evil 4, Ark survival ascensed..... y otros a los que dedico horas, sí que se benefician claramente de contar con suficiente VRAM.

Por eso, si tu objetivo es jugar en 1080p y gráficos medios, probablemente con 12 GB tendrás suficiente hasta con 8 Vram. Pero si hablamos de 2K, 4K, VR o IA local, creo que el mínimo realista a día de hoy es 16 GB de VRAM en adelante, lo suyo 20 VRAM, también los que compramos una 5080 (por ejemplo) pagamos 1500 euros, para que te den 16 miseros  de VRAM .

Buenas vengo a dar un poco de luz sobre la info q se da en el video , el comentario va a ser un poco complicado de explicar y de entender . Lo primero es q lo q se dice en el video es una verdad parcial ya q se manipula la información para demostrar un punto sin decir toda la verdad .
Aclaraciones  :
- Para q un juego corra bien de forma fluida en un PC tiene q llegar min a los 60 fps de media por debajo de eso el juego no corre de una forma aceptable . 
- No podemos incluir el DLSS ya q este es una tecnología de reescalado por lo q disminuye el uso de la VRAM .
- Tenemos q usar todo en lo máximo y usar  ray tracing  o path tracing en caso de estar disponible  todo a la máxima expresión 
Tenemos como conclusiones q para q se cumpla la afirmación " los 12 Gb de la RTX son suficientes para jugar todo los juegos a 1440" tiene q cumplirse las distintas condiciones :
Tiene q ser capas de correr los juegos a 60 fps de media ( mínimo ) , con la mayor calidad grafica posible , sin usar DLSS o bajar gráficos .
Como conclusión tenemos de q no es posible  como mismo tu lo demostraste en tu video no corre algunos juegos a 60 fps . 

Ahora empecemos con la segunda parte del comentario :
¿ xq los juegos si consumen menos de 12 Gb de VRAM no van a 60 fps en mi 5070 ?
La respuesta esta en la construcción de los videojuegos  y en el uso del cache (el cual en este video no se explica muy bien ) . La mayoría de videojuegos ya casi no se optimizan xq existe el DLSS dándole a los creadores de videojuegos una nueva comodidad y dándonos a nosotros como usuarios una nueva realidad a la hora de jugar q es : ¡tienes q usar reescalado para jugar a videojuegos !

¿ Xq necesitas 16 Gb en mi 5070 para poder disfrutar del máximo de los videojuegos en 1440 ?
 🤣🤣 Lamento decirte q no son suficientes ya q solo ganarías mas cache y unos pocos fps ( pocos = 1 - 5 aprox ).
El problema esta en la construcción de los videojuegos y q no tenemos un Hardware lo suficiente mente veloz para alcanzar las anteriores condiciones (En la 5070 ) . La carrera en la industria del videojuego por el foto realismo nos a llevado a un tope el cual vamos a superar gracias a la IA , permitiéndonos así obtener mejores gráficos y mas fluidez .

Conclusión : no son suficientes en TODOS los juegos con esas condiciones xq no es la intención y xq la 5070 no tiene la velocidad y la potencia de computo requería para esa tarea independientemente de su cantidad de VRAM .

Pero haber, es que yo creo y entiendo por donde van los tiros. No tiene ningun sentido tener más VRAM si luego la GPU no te tira del juego. 

Yo tengo una 2080 ti que en su día, era una bestia, pero hoy una 4060 da igual rendimiento, y ni hablar si ponemos dlss y rt.

Mi GPU tiene 11gb de VRAM pero el problema es que aunque tenga memoria para tirar de la mayoría de juegos a 1440p en la máxima calidad, es absurdo, ya que la tarjeta no tiene potencia para tirar de los gráficos, se queda antes corto el chip que la VRAM.

Y luego está el tema de los monitores. Los que habéis probado jugar en un monitor 144 o mas Hz de refresco, seguramente no queráis volver a tener 60. Es que no es ni remotamente parecido, yo intento bajar siempre los ajustes para intentar al menos llegar a 90-100 fps mínimos, que es cuando se empieza a ver realmente suave.

Por no decir que la jugabilidad en cuanto a latencias con mas fps disminuye en gran medida, lo que nos da una experiencia mucho mejor.

Entoces a lo que voy, tiene sentido que la 5070 tenga 12gb? Totalmente, tiene todo el sentido al menos para mí, ya que si queremos alcanzar al menos esos 90-100 fps, vamos a tener que bajar la calidad gráfica sí o sí, y entonces bajará el consumo de VRAM.

Ahora bien, tiene sentido que al precio que vale un modulo de VRAM no le hayan puesto 16 o más? Totalmente, creo que el precio hubiera variado unos euros, pero ya os digo yo que si Nvidia le pone 16gb a la 5070, AMD iba a vender pocas 9070 y xt.

Fallo total por parte de NVIDIA, que por no gastarse unos euros más, está perdiendo contra la competencia, al menos en ventas.

Sinceramente creo que esta generación es de paso, para el que tenga cualquier gráfica que rinda más o menos bien, yo no actualizaría, porque el salto generacional es con las rtx 6000 de nvidia y las rx 10000 de amd, que es cuando va a haber competencia en el tema de IA.

Hasta entonces, yo seguiré con mi 2080ti, pero si tuviera que comprar una GPU hoy dia, seria la 5070, creo que es la única que precio rendimiento para juegos es interesante. Además es una GPU que con undervolt consume 150w y al precio que está la luz hoy en dia, me parece una compra inteligente por relación precio-rendimiento-consumo.

04:00 La caché, ya es memoria en uso, esto debido a que el juego o la aplicación que estés ejecutando, la esta usando para precargar archivos allí que es probable que los use en determinado momento, los carga en cache para tenerlos  a la mano, ya que si el juego o la aplicación los necesita, la grafica no tiene por que recurrir al CPU o SSD a buscarlos nuevamente, en el caso de los videojuegos, se mantienen precargadas texturas en cache para agilizar la jugabilidad no tener latencia o tirones.

Hace más de 15 años ya pasaba lo mismo: Ya estaba en el mercardo la legendaria 8800 GT con 512 MB era una bestia (cómo se veía el primer AC con esta gráfica, uuuuf). Pero llegó la serie 9000, y con ella la 9400 GT de 1 GB, que se vendió como pan caliente. ¿La razón? era porque... "¡Tiene 1 GB!", decían, porque si la PC iba como un rayo con 2 GB de ram, se asumía que con 1 GB en la gráfica, la PC iba a trascender a otra dimensión.

La verdad de Nvidia es que al tener una memoria vram muy rapida puede ahorrar mas memoria, el problema es que al hacerlo sufre de acceder de nuevo al caché de memoria para volver a recompilar las texturas y el sombreado y es lo que genera los fps 0.1% o el minimo 1% o mas bien llamado caida de frames o el famoso stuttering o tartamudeo, eso es lo q Nvidia no entiende y lo viene haciendo desde la Gtx 900 1000 y rtx 2000 3000 4000 y 5000 pero bueno los fanboys como son tan ciegos, Amd tiene la tecnología Rbar que hace que utilice toda la Vram que necesita el juego para poder generar fotogramas sin necesidad de volver al caché y volver a recompilar como hace Nvidia y entonces cuando ves los videos de Nvidia contra AMD notas una gigante diferencia de tartamudeo de Nvidia y se ve en su frame time es es muy parecido al electro cardiograma, los saltos y picos es el tartamudeo pero como youtube suele usar en video progresivo no suele notarse los frames frenados y tartamudeo, entonces ese misma linea de frame rate te avisa cuando sucede y cuantos fps de caida inestables tienes. Y si tener mayor memoria Vram evitas la compilación de shaders y caché como lo hace Nvidia y evitas bajos rendimientos y tartamudeo constantes, aca no gana lo potencia bruta, gana quien mejor Vram tiene tu gráfica, antes quizás no era tan necesario, pero hoy en dia si lo es, era cuestión de esperar a que Nvidia termine con esa mentira de tener memorias mejores por ser de tecnología de punta, una gddr6 comun supera a en rendimiento bruto a una gddr7 y no por velocidad y frecuencia sino xq al tener baja Vram no puedes hacer magia de ahorrar y se te satura

El hecho de que estemos debatiendo si son suficientes 12 gb de vram para principalmente correr juegos que son inferiores artisticamente a productos como red dead redemtion 2, cyberpunk 2077 , god of war, Horizon zero dawn o Elden Ring demuestra el nivel masivo de cagada en el que esta metido el desarrollo de videojuegos en los ultimos 2 a;os. JUegos con apenas algun tipo de avance e inferiores en la mayoria de los aspectos a sus antecesores en ps4, como si alguno de estos productos minimamente por esto pudieran tener la calidad de un juego de ps3 como por ejemplo GTA V. Pidiendo equipos con cantidades ingentes de memoria como si se notara algun tipo de cambio. Este es uno de los motivos del porque cada producto que sacan es fracaso tras fracaso en la industria AAA y del porque el 95% de la gente en steam solo juega juegos de hace 10 a;os.

Las grandes desarrolladoras de juegos actualmente se han vuelto firmas que desarrollan simplemente programas para bechmarks de PC en vez de videojuegos. En vez de centrarse en que sus juegos corran facilmente a 60 fps en las consolas actuales.

Cuando tu juego de 200 gb en su estreno se juega menos que por ejemplo Metal Gear Solid V en steam, deberias plantearte todo el proceso de desarrollo del videojuego

por lo que tengo entendigo, hay que entender que el ancho de banda de la memoria es muy importante , un ejemplo claro fue la generacion pasada de amd como la r9 fury .....Bus de Memoria Enormemente Ancho.A diferencia de la memoria GDDR5 utilizada en muchas tarjetas contemporáneas con un bus más estrecho (típicamente de 256 o 384 bits), la R9 Fury incorporaba un bus de memoria masivo de 4096 bits. Imagínalo como tener una súper autopista en lugar de una carretera normal; mucha más información puede viajar simultáneamente.incluso con una velocidad de reloj relativamente más baja en comparación con la GDDR5, resultó en un ancho de banda de memoria increíblemente alto: 512 GB/s. Esto era significativamente superior al de tarjetas de la competencia como la NVIDIA GTX 980, que tenía alrededor de 224 GB/s de ancho de banda de memoria

En resumen, mientras el ancho de banda sea mas grande pues no importa que tanta cantidad de VRAM q  tenga en algunos casos ojo

FYI in the Indiana Jones game, the ID Tech engine is made to basically dump everything into VRAM to reduce pop in and stutter, that's why the VRAM requirements are crazy like that. If you don't meet the VRAM's demands, it will simply streams in lower-res textures first and replace them with higher-res ones later or sometimes it never loads the highest tier if VRAM is too limited. It's just their engine and how it works, it won't in a sense "crash" your game like most engines when you overload your card with VRAM assets. I only know this because I was shocked that this game required so much VRAM so I researched it.

If it is just an ordinary RTX 4070 ti 12gb, not RTX 4070 ti super 16gb, then it's not that much better than RTX 5070. Go with 5070, go with newer card, because future games will be more stable on more recent hardware and you'll have driver support for a bit longer. 5070 out of the box is around 5% weaker than 4070ti stock. However, 5070, as any 50 series gpu for this matter, is a very good overclocker. Nvidia, for some reason, didn't push them to their true potential. I overclocked my 5070 to 3000mhz and added +2000mhz to vram clock speed, i am pretty sure it's now faster than any 4070ti. You can basically get  +10% free FPS for 5070. (It was super easy for my first overclocking experience, i just followed first YT guide i found)

However, if you can find RTX 4070 ti super in the same price range, grab it immediately, disregard everything i just said, it's completely different story. Ti super has 16 gb of vram, this alone beats everything. It's way more important than multi frame gen, and DLSS 4 upscaling is actually available on all RTX cards, not just the latest. It's only multi frame generation that is locked to 50 series. Don't be confused

So:
RTX 4070 ti super > RTX 5070 > RTX 4070 ti

The thing most people forget (or are unaware of) is the shift from DDR6 to DDR7 VRAM in the 5000-series GPUs. The reason they did that is specifically for VRAM overhead to support 4K+ and Ray Tracing, etc.

Everyone has it in their heads that "12GB won't be enough" and that's false, because it's only based in the experience of DDR6. "12GB of DDR6" might not be enough in prior generations, and could technically be maxed out TWICE as fast as DDR7, but really 12GB of DDR7 is equivalent to 24GB of DDR6.

DDR6 has a max data transfer rate of 20-24GB/s, versus DDR7's 32-48GB/s, which equates to needing less VRAM to accomplish the same overhead. That translates to a bandwidth of 504GB/s in the old DDR6, compared to 1TB/s with DDR7 and a 256-384-bit bus. DDR7 is also 50% faster in signaling per clock cycle, so it also doesn't need to have as high of a clock speed as DDR6. DDR7's integrated AI also actively manages TENSOR cores, so it's capable of real-time AI rendering and inference. It's technology that's developed for 4K and scalable to 8K gaming.

Even AMD's new 7900 XTX and 9070 XT are still on DDR6 and that's why they have 16-24GB. - They need it to accomplish the same thing as 12GB of DDR7 from Nvidia's 5000-series.

I agree the 5070 is the worst gpu in the 5000 series generation, but being a 5000 series card, it still offers a lot of things that amd can't match. One of which is 4x frame generation, which I know a lot of people hate on but if you actually go and look at the benchmarks between 4x frame gen and 2x/ no frame generation on the 5070 you'll find that the input latency difference between them are almost negligible, in some cases actually being lower WITH frame generation than without due to nvidia reflex. You really have to try it for yourself to see, it's not nearly as bad as people make it out to be. You're essentially getting double or triple the frame rates with almost no noticeable downsides.
The second is far better ray tracing on the nvidia cards compared to amd cards. This is most apparent in black myth wukong because it has some of the most intense ray tracing out of any game, where the 9070 has half the fps of even the 5070. Amd has done a good job closing the gap in games with lower ray tracing levels, but with more and more games coming up with ray tracing enabled by default it's not a very futureproof option.
You speak of 90% of cases, but raw, rasterized performance is actually the 10% here, almost everyone uses upscaling in this day and age, almost all new games have ray tracing, and even frame generation is starting to become more accepted. Nvidia gpus are better in all three of these areas. 
If you don't care about gaming with the latest features, or maybe you just play competitive shooters or whatnot, and you're on a tight budget, then yes the 9070 and 9070 xt are both good choices, but don't try to pretend you can just buy the same/ better product for less if you switch to amd lol, the rtx 5000 series and rx 9000 series are rly not comparable at all. 
Not hating on amd or anything, I think they're doing great in the cpu market (low bar though considering what intel has offered lately). Best pairing atm is an amd cpu with an nvidia gpu.

The 5070 is honestly far better. People will whine about the VRAM, until you realize that almost no game will use 12GB VRAM at 1440p now or for very long down the road. Unless you literally use 4K texture pack/mods which can potentially push VRAM usage up past 12GB on a small amount of games. But then on the 5060 TI the performance will tank because it can't handle it with the 128-bit bus width and it gets crushed at 4K as well, which means you will be dropping settings for higher FPS sooner (which also lowers VRAM usage). If the 5060 TI was under $400 it might make sense, but a lot of times I've seen it for $450 and the 5070 for $500-$525, making the 5070 a vastly superior option for 1440p or even 4K gaming.

It is just a hard comparison, if the 5070 base had 16gb and was like 100 dollars more in real life pricing, then the nobrainer is to get the 5070 but that isn't the case. 12GB for most games at this point in time is good enough for high settings, but it is concerning that a new card that varies so wildly in price is hitting the limit in VRAM and crashing in certain games like Indiana Jones. This is now, down the line it could get worst or you can hope that developers optimize their games better (good luck). 

There are also the cases where the card isn't only used for Gaming, but for other workloads as well that could benefit from more VRAM like LLMs, affects editing, etc... (Hobbyist Work)

If you are a person that would keep a card for a long time, the answer isn't clear between these two models. One is more powerful but can be hampered by VRAM while the other has less performance. If you plan to sell and upgrade regularly, get the 5070. If price is no issue, get the 5070ti for gaming, 5080 for gaming/workload.

5070 has 192bit memory interface, 5060 has just default 128bit. This means 5070 has higher transfer rate through dedicated circuit lanes between GPU and memory chips. So the less VRAM amount is compensated by transfer rate. But at the end, it's obviously just another marketing strategy (scam) by nVidia: they could have made 5070 having 16GB VRAM instead of 12GB for that price (around 130$ more). 5060 works at 8x PCIe speed, while 5070 at 16x. Even if it's a very small performance difference (2-4%), nVidia usually is missing (probably on purpose) to mention it.
AMD copied this scam as well for their 7700 XT models, giving them just 12GB VRAM and 192bit interface, but they run the GDDR6 version still, while nVidia 5060-5070 models have already GDDR7.

As for Frame Generation 3× or 4×, it’s hardly a playable experience. If it truly delivered smooth gameplay without the crazy lag, no one with a 30-series card would bother upgrading—they’d just enable FSR 3 frame gen 4× and call it a day. Extra VRAM alone doesn’t equal better performance; raw power and actual frame output are what count.

Indiana Jones Texture Cache setting is like the slider for texture pool size in DOOM TDA. You won't see any difference on screen between med to supreme. Also, 9070/9070xt for path tracing? Hahah. RDNA4 doesn’t have ser, omm, or proper denoiser to even have path tracing work functionally. This is why rdna4 is already doa if you want to do path tracing and even ray tracing in specific titles, and it will only get worse when rdna5 comes out as it will have better instruction sets. How can you guys mislead your community so much?

People are over hyping the VRAM topic. Sure, more is in theory always better, but additional VRAM is like money on your bank account that you do not spend. If you don't need the extra VRAM, it does nothing for you. You can look at it, but a game that needs 7GB of VRAM will run the same on a 5060ti 8GB and 16GB. 
If you are lacking compute power or DLSS features, no amount of VRAM helps you. If you run out of VRAM by a few GB, there is typically a single setting to remedy this, with little loss of visual quality.  If you you are at 100% GPU usage, extra VRAM does nothing.

It is a no-brainer question, 5070 all day long. Look, how much VRAM you have is only relevant if your GPU has the juice to use it. 16GB on a weak GPU, such as the RTX 5060 TI/ RX9060 XT, is BS. You're basically paying extra for 4GB of VRAM you will never be able to use.

On the GDD6 vs GDDR7 question. The truth is the difference in performance between the two is almost identical. I mean the 5070ti isn't running any faster is it? lol 
GDDR7 uses a novel 3 tick cycle which is prone to a higher error rate. This isn't an issue as GDDR7 has built in ECC. It is correcting its errors as they happen. So in REALITY in operation 7 isn't actually much faster. Yes it has more on paper bandwidth, but in reality you have to factor in lost cycles for ECC correction, which isn't an issue with 6. The actual real world difference in bandwidth is not high. Put that ram in a PC with 7 or 8 noise creating fans and you will see even more fails. When Nvidia first started using multi cycle ram techniques in servers they had to physically move the vram away from active cooling solutions to avoid ECC cycles killing performance. GDDR7 isn't actually any better is the point.

The issue is GPU configuration and memory bus width. You can't put 16gb on a 192 bit bus (like 5070). In order for 5070 to NOT have a 192 bit bus they would have to completely reconfigure the GPU. Basically their whole GPU design and product stack decisions have led to these VRAM issues. They could go with 3gb modules instead of 2gb, but those are apparently a lot more expensive. That would result in an 18gb 5070.

@hartsickdisciple  that is the case, but historically the xx70 was always 256bit. NVIDIA’s decision to attempt to backtrack to 192bit for the xx70 GPUs for the last two generations is what got them into that problem, not some inherent limit of technology. 

Also, every refresh for the last the last three NVIDIA generations has included increasing the bus size on at least one of the GPUs, so it’s hardly impossible for them to do that again this generation for the 5070.

I literally have a 2060 8gb card. It still works, it still plays, and it still plays at 1440p, which is what I have. I find 4k to be a gimmick, even after experiencing it at a friend's house, from a gaming perspective, but NOT for streaming services/movie perspective. Remember, a game is vastly more than its textures where as movies and streaming of video content is a purely visual art form, and some of the best I've played are 2d isometric games that were recent releases. We're talking about running a low end card on modern games that was two generations older than what most of these games were even designed for, and it still played. Not on ultramax, super 4k eyegasm graphics, but it highlighted the key features, the core mechanics of the games and still ran them at a respectable 60FPS at 1440p. The problem is that too many people and far too many graphics companies are pushing their high tier stuff when they should be pushing and supporting the lower to mid range the most. If intel pulls that W in the budget graphics market and AMD keeps up with the demand at the prices they're offering, the only thing Nvidia will be able to sell is their high tier cards, because blow to blow, the recent AMD cards keep up, and price per frame, they do better. Nvidia won't even be able to keep the high end market as AMD begins to release their high end cards to compete because, once again as proven by the last generation, blow for blow they keep up, and price per frame is vastly better. Hell, I can go play the new 40k game on low and still manage it it 1440p (Now, I do overclock my cards, but that just proves that even on the low end the sheer life you can get out of these things is beyond impressive.) and it will play at industry standard, which I know is a low bar to pass, but it's still INDUSTRY STANDARD. It's not about defending 8gb, it's about decrying the industry for focusing on making the game pretty so that people ignore how genuinely bad most AAA titles are these days. @haukikannel  or the AMD SSD as VRAM monstrosity. VRAM is practically useless without bandwidth to back it up, and a single SoDIMM is in the 30-50GB/s range if I remember correctly. Whereas the weakest 128bit GPUs still have 270+GB/s of bandwidth. Not to mention that a single SoDIMM is 64bit and so you’d be limited to two slots worth of bandwidth on these entry level GPUs, and only 8 with a theoretical 400GB/s of bandwidth on 5090s whereas the real deal has 2TB/s of bandwidth. It’s the fundamental limitation behind desktop iGPUs in anything other than a ThreadRipper socket. Stryx Halo for example uses to equivalent of 4 SoDIMM slots on its 256Bit bus (except soldered) and is only able to compete with the 128Bit GPUs. In no small part because that’s what GPUs have equal VRAM bandwidth. Obviously, Stryx Halo does win in scenarios where you need more than the 8GB those 128Bit laptops are limited to however.

Y'all really dont understand how VRAM and bus width works. Every GPU die can only have a few VRAM configurations. RTX 5070's GB205 die has connections only for 192 bit bus, this means only 6, 12, 24 GB configurations are possible with 2 GB memory modules. RTX 5060 Ti's GB206 die has connections only for 128 bit bus, this means only 4, 8 or 16 GB configurations are possible with 2 GB memory modules. 5060 Ti creates its 16 GB configuration by using 4 more memory modules on the back of the PCB. Thus is has four 2 GB modules on the front and four on the back giving 16 GB of total VRAM. 5070 has six 2 GB modules on the front giving a total of 12 GB of VRAM. 5070 Ti, 5080 and RX 9070/XT have 256 bit bus which means by using eight 2 GB modules on the front they get 16 GB. Every memory module gets 32 lanes of the memory bus in all of these configurations.

This compares the 5060 TI to a regular 5070 (non-TI). The 5070 TI also has 16GB of VRAM.

It makes more sense if you understand how graphics cards work. There are three main factors that contribute to performance: Graphics Processor speed, VRAM buffer, and Memory Bus Width. All three of those things work in conjuction with each other to produce frames. The 5070 has a faster processor (GB205) which has 6144 cores. The 5060 ti uses the GB206 which has only 4608 cores. The 5070's memory bus width is 192-bit, vs only 128-bit for the 5060 ti. With a faster processor and wider memory bus, the 5070 doesn't need as much VRAM buffer to render scenes and generate frames. The memory bandwidth of these two cards is 672 GB/s (5070) vs 448 GB/s (5060 ti). The memory bus is lanes of data traffic between the memory chips and the gpu. Larger amounts of VRAM can help make up for a slower GPU to ensure smooth frame generation by storing data like scene textures to VRAM instead of having to retrieve it from system memory.  I don't need to watch this video to tell you which card is better: the 5070. Faster processor with more cores, wider memory bus. My 3080 is two generations older than the 5060 ti, has less VRAM, but is still more than 20% faster because it has a 384-bit memory bus and 8906 cores. Its memory bandwidth is 912.4 GB/s, more than double the 5060 ti's. It uses an older gpu architecture, older generation of VRAM, and still kicks the 5060 ti's ass. VRAM counts for something, but it's not everything that goes into the performance level of a graphics card.

His comment about the next console generation being designed around higher amounts of Vram kinda overlooks how things are kind of unique right now when it comes to memory manufacturing with no end in sight. 

Previous hardware shortages didn't have all of the manufacturers making the components cutting their production or even (in the case of crucial) shutting down their consumer brand entirely so it was clear that things would inevitably improve and the shortage will end. With companies moving more and more of their production capacity to datacenter ram, unless someone changes their mind and goes back or someone opens a company specifically for consumer applications of memory then the supply shortage isn't going to improve significantly any time soon.

PT was playable without RR at 1440p/DLSS Performance on the 3080Ti, but after only a few minutes of gameplay, the game would stutter, which manifests into a framedrops every second until you disable and re-enable RT. Greetings from Iran, Daniel. 5070 is the equivalent of 4070 ti (I have a 4070 ti). Both have roughly the exact same power and the same amount of VRAM. Based on my experience 12 GBs of VRAM is quite enough for current games such as Indiana Jones with full path tracing at 1440p. The cards with 12 GBs of VRAM are 1440p cards not 4K cards, and at that resolution you don't need to set textures to ultra. You can play with every setting maxed out with full path tracing except for textures that you need to set to high and get over 60 FPS easily. 16 GBs of VRAM is for 4K gaming with every setting including textures maxed out and it is at 4k resolution that you will actually see a difference by setting the texture settings from high to ultra or supreme, not at 1440p. A weaker card such as 5060 ti can't utilize its 16 GBs of VRAM efficiently because you'll be getting abysmal FPS at maxed out settings, but a 5070 / 4070 ti can easily handle games at maxed out settings even with 12 GBs of VRAM. If I had to choose between the two cards, I'd buy the one with more raw power, because that's what will get you higher FPS, not the VRAM that it can't even utilize unless you settle for 30 FPS instead of 60.

P.S. You might not have noticed it, but in Indiana Jones, setting Vegetation Animation Quality from ultra / high to medium will boost your FPS dramatically and that setting is not even dependent on VRAM. Nobody seems to have talked about it until now.

These weird memory configs are purely due to bus width limitations.

192bit bus of the 5070 needs 6 memory chips whilst the 128bit bus of the 5060ti only needs 4.

You then have to come up with a memory configuration using only 2, 3 or 4gb memory chips.

You could use a combination to get the desired memory config, but we all know what happened with the gtx970 don't we...
The 5070 use 3gb modules. So it cannot have 16. Only 15 or 18

I honestly don't think you'll run into VRAM problems this soon. It's taken like 10 years for 8GB to be a 'problem' for some people. @MMillerPCs
I seriously need someone to point out the greed of customers upgrading every year as the #1 reason for price increase of the last 2.5 decades.

It's not hard to look at the sales starting in 2000 and seeing consumer upgrades happened once every 4 years as a "leap frog" consumption.

Then when the 10 series dropped in 2016, it changed. Upgrades were happening every 18 months. Meaning consumers are now demanding access to the latest and greatest every 2 years.

In 2020 with the release of the 30 series cards prices sky rocketed compared to the 20 series. Yet the performance was rather minimum. Did that stop consumers? Nah... they demanded upgrades.

Once the 40 series hit, the demand for sub components hit and the price took off again. Yet the performance increase was certainly better than the 20 series but, not worth the cost as an upgrade from the 30 series.

Same issues came up upgrading from the 40 to the 50. Only this time, Nvidia didn't exactly have anything new to offer. Just the same 40 series with more ram and slightly better power efficiency.

So why upgrade from the 40 series? Just stop soaking up the lastest and greatest, skip a generation or two. Our wallets will thank us for it.

for the people who play CS2, it is important to notice 2 things: 1. it's a cpu based game (which he mentioned); 2. he played death match on 1440p max settings, which means that, if you take this down to popular competitive res's (such as 1280*960 4:3, or even just take it down to standard 1920*1080p), lower down the graphics a bit (which is what the vast majority of people do), and go into a normal competitive match, your fps count will be way higher. Of course, how much higher it will be depends on what cpu you have. Hope I helped some of those CS2 comp nerds like me lmao. Also, for CS2 benchmarks, I recommend using a workshop map called "CS2 FPS BENCHMARK DUST2" instead of playing a deathmatch.

I honestly don't think the RTX 5070 is as bad as people make it out to be, IF you can get it for MSRP or at least reasonably close to there. Is it amazing or overwhelming? No, not really. But it seems to be quite close to the 9070 XT in some scenarios, and is surprisingly not that far off from the 9070-non XT even without ray-tracing. The 12GB VRAM on it is honestly fine as well, considering you can lower textures to High if necessary and they still look very good; in action or real-world gameplay you really won't notice the difference. By the time 16GB becomes a requirement for medium texture quality, it is likely the 9070/XT won't have enough compute power to play modern titles well. The RTX 5070 is also more energy efficient than AMD's offerings, which is good if you live in a warm/hot climate and/or use it in a small space. I don't have one, but this is based on my educated understanding and with reviews.

The whole YouTube and reddit hivemind about 8gb being all but unplayable and 12gb cards being foolish to buy is MASSIVELY overblown. For example, I bought a FTW3 3070, which is 8GB, in 2021 and I am still using it today, playing in 4K on a 65" OLED, and it has provided playable framerates in ALL of the 100+ games I've thrown at it. Not once have I had to go back to my 1440p monitor. There was really only one game where I had to run mostly medium settings and that was Indiana Jones, likely bcuz ray tracing is baked in for its global illumination and therefore can't even be run on a 10-series card. But with a combo of mid and high settings, I was able to play at 4k with playable frames (I played on 30fps consoles for decades and not elitist, so anything consistently above 50fps, pref 60 is objectively playable, at least for casual SP games - I aim for 90+ in MP games) and the game still looked great. Ultra settings are suuuper wasteful, as proven in many studies where pro tech content creators/gamers couldn't notice any difference between ultra and high side-by-side. I play most games at around 80% high settings, with textures always on high, shadows often on medium. I've lately been playing Clair Obscur, RE4 Remake, The Last of Us 1 + 2. GoW Ragnarok, Cyberpunk, Dead Space, RDR2, CoD:BO6  and Atomfall - and I'm getting at least 60fps in all of them, with most being closer to 80-100fps, and all of them with mostly high settings and upscaling on quality or balanced. I mean I was playing Sifu the other day and maxing out my 120hz refresh rate with no upscaling and max settings. So while I def agree that BUYING an 8gb card now for anything other than 1080p is not a good idea, all the people whining about anything less than 16gb being a non-starter are just foolish, ESPECIALLY considering that >70% of gamers are playing at 1080/1440p, with 60% STILL at 1080p. If I'm still getting at least 50-60+ in everything with an 8gb card at 4k AND a paltry 4-core 3300x, then 12gb will be fine for everyone who doesn't insist on native 4k and getting 100+ fps

The issue with 12gb VRAM is that it affects performance as well. 

Less VRAM means less headroom for textures to be cached, this translates to microstutters or bad 1% lows since cached assets need to be replaced sooner.

12gb VRAM is still viable and sure you can still run the latest games @ 1440p Ultra, however, if you are playing raster heavy games, a smooth seamless experience may not be expected and additional tweaks may be needed to optimize your settings. E.g. My 16gb 6800xt can smoothly run Hunt Showdown, without stutters when entering new areas. The same cannot be said for my buddy's 8gb 5060ti and he has a 9800x3d vs my 7800x3d.

The whole YouTube and reddit hivemind about 8gb being all but unplayable and 12gb cards being foolish to buy is MASSIVELY overblown. For example, I bought a FTW3 3070, which is 8GB, in 2021 and I am still using it today, playing in 4K on a 65" OLED, and it has provided playable framerates in ALL of the 100+ games I've thrown at it. Not once have I had to go back to my 1440p monitor. There was really only one game where I had to run mostly medium settings and that was Indiana Jones, likely bcuz ray tracing is baked in for its global illumination and therefore can't even be run on a 10-series card. But with a combo of mid and high settings, I was able to play at 4k with playable frames (I played on 30fps consoles for decades and not elitist, so anything consistently above 50fps, pref 60 is objectively playable, at least for casual SP games - I aim for 90+ in MP games) and the game still looked great. Ultra settings are suuuper wasteful, as proven in many studies where pro tech content creators/gamers couldn't notice any difference between ultra and high side-by-side. I play most games at around 80% high settings, with textures always on high, shadows often on medium. I've lately been playing Clair Obscur, RE4 Remake, KCD2, The Last of Us 1+2. GoW Ragnarok, Cyberpunk, Dead Space, RDR2, CoD:BO6  and Atomfall - and I'm getting at least 60fps in all of them, with most being closer to 80-100fps, and all of them with mostly high settings and upscaling on quality or balanced. I mean I was playing Sifu the other day and maxing out my 120hz refresh rate with no upscaling and max settings. 

So while I def agree that BUYING an 8gb card now for anything other than 1080p is not a good idea, all the people whining about anything less than 16gb being a non-starter are just foolish, ESP considering that >70% of gamers are playing at 1080/1440p, with 60% STILL at 1080p. If I'm still getting at least 50-60+ in everything with an 8gb card at 4k AND a paltry 4-core 3300x, then 12gb and a solid 6-8-core CPU will be fine for everyone who doesn't insist on native 4k while expecting 100+ fps. Have you even used a 12gb card or just jumped on the hivemind bandwagon? Because I just got a 5070 and it's crushing everything I'm throwing at it in 4k, around 50-60% higher fps than my 3070, and wayyy more with FG/MFG. 12gb is perfectly fine, esp as upscaling/AI tech improve (which it will as it makes up 85% of Nvidia's revenue) and you upgrade to a new card every 2-3 gens like most consumers. Upgrading gen to gen is dumb AF with GPUs, same as it is with phones. Very few people are keeping their GPUs for more than 5yrs, so by the time 12gb isn't enough, they'll be upgrading anyway. I mean hell, my buddy is currently playing KCD2 with a 6gb 2060 KO and is enjoying his experience, so all you goofballs saying 12gb VRAM is not enough to enjoy your games for at least 3yrs are just regurgitating the nonsense you hear and not basing your opinion on real world experiences, just being sheep like most people and believing whatever you're told to believe, like all the morons that voted for mango Mussolini and believed all his blatant lies that he'd lower prices, end wars in 24hrs and fix everything lol 🤷‍♂

Should have done this on FE models only. Can't really reproduce performance reliably across AIB models. TuF are probably the most basic featured over cooled cards produced. 

Also: If the only thing changing between the models is the number of FPS you are getting... that's to be expected if Nvidia is putting out a new GPU. People don't play games like Cyberpunk at 15fps. 

When will people who do benchmarks/reviews learn to control for testing or stop over emphasizing specific model cadence. Putting all the RTX xx70 cards against each other is one task. Getting similar FPS from them all is another task. Testing FE models exclusively to control for AIB cooler variations and custom power profiles... using the same core system to test with (and correctly matching it with a xx70 class card, not doing stupid hardware unboxed stuff like using the most powerful CPU to test a 5050). 

If you're not going to put in the effort to isolate a card to it's best case scenario and then tune settings to achieve it's best usage case, you're really just not testing anything. One needs to recognize the difference between gen 3/4/5, the game support optimizations present for a card, and also drivers. 

Using the most recent driver with a 3070 results in some pretty wonky behavior. Using an older driver, the best known driver for a 3070, would result in much better performance... and then the 5070 might not run on that same driver. Or the game chosen might not run without going past 582 drivers. 

It's never as simple as putting X Card against Y card. 

Here's a more realistic breakdown: 

The 2070 represents a small jump from the 1070, with RTX added (even though it's horrendous). It's basically a step below so many other cards one could get for either way cheaper price or much better performance. I would be better off with a 12GB 3060 or a 1080 ti than a 2070. I'm not using RTX or DLSS or any of that nonsense at this performance level, anyway. 

The 3070 represents a significant jump over the 2070, both in terms of RT and Raster performance. It's solidly 160% of a 2070, in a single generation (almost unheard of). I almost wouldn't even call the 2070 an RTX card or even in the same 'league' of cards as the 3070, 4070, and 5070. 

The 4070 is a 3070 Super at just 30% more powerful, using the same manufacturing node (basically better binned rather than a true generational leap). Buying a 4070 would be the same choice as buying a 3080 or a 6900XT. In fact, a used 3080 would be a better upgrade (depending on model, Vram amount) over any RTX 4070 (the 4070 is basically a suped up 3060 12GB). While the lower end performance metrics would line up well, at the higher end, under load, a 3080 is a better card than a 4070. Even the 10GB version. 

The 5070 is the most generationally confused out of the whole bunch. It is literally just a faster version of the 4070 GPU slapped onto the same architecture they have recycled since the RTX 3060 (192 bit 12GB Vram) but the chip is even more fasterer. Logic would tell you picking up an RTX 3060 12GB is nearly the same purchasing choice as getting a 4070 or a 5070, just spend more if you have more to spend and can find a card. 

If we are having a discussion about FPS, most gamers aren't trying to play at 1440p. Mathematically, 1080p (or another upscaled lower res) is going to perform better for a game, leading to higher FPS than 1440p. Do people play at 1440p? Sure. That's a tradeoff of  FPS for image quality.  Your entire video is trading FPS for slightly better image quality. 

I'd be interested to see what you get at 1080p across these 4 cards, I'd be interested to see the architectures tested side by side (3060 12GB vs 4070 vs 5070) and the understanding cards such as the 3070 are truly rare (both the 4070 and 5070 have smaller bus width than the 3070, despite having more Vram, going to result in worse performance at same settings). 

Finally, max FPS =/= stable FPS. Gaming is all about stable, fluid motion, clear, detailed scene. There still is no substitute for rasterized 1080p performance on maxxed settings. DLSS on a 5050 or 5060 is a crime.

It's important to understand the difference between absolute gains and relative gains.  Absolute gains may be about the same but relative gains will drop as performance increases.  A 10fps increase is 50% when you were running at 20fps in 2018.  The same 10fps is 10% when you are running at 100fps.  So relative gains will drop and features will increase.  That sums up the evolution of the 70 series cards with the exception of the double-gains from 2070 to 3070.

That's fine, but don't write it off permanently. Every few months or so the image quality of both upscaling and frame generation is improving, and the latency penalties are broadly decreasing. And as this video shows, in some games the latency difference between FG off and on is imperceptibly low, only 1ms in a few cases. And if your monitor supports a higher refresh rate, multi-frame gen can actually do a lot more to improve image quality than you think. Going from 80fps to 200+ fps can add a degree of motion clarity that makes up for other areas of reduced IQ. There are more trade-offs now than strictly "better vs worse" and every weakness of the technology is improving drastically. Just a few years ago people wouldn't use DLSS because it was too much of a problem for IQ. Now there are legit debates on whether DLSS exceeds native in image quality, or whether people can even tell the difference in some titles between native or even DLAA on vs DLSS Quality or even Balanced.

@ASalad  I agree on your points. I am a BIG fan of DLAA and think it looks noticeably better than native in almost every game. One downside is how demanding it is. Even with a 4090 a handful of games can struggle to run it or require you to use frame gen. With this new update 4.5 I find that DLSS Quality now gives an even better image than 4.0 DLAA did. So now you can run DLSS Quality and still get an even better DLAA image but no longer need the use of frame gen for many systems. As DLSS progresses that should become more and more true and be more and more available to lower spec systems. I am excited for the future of DLSS.

Should have done this on FE models only. Can't really reproduce performance reliably across AIB models. TuF are probably the most basic featured over cooled cards produced. 

Also: If the only thing changing between the models is the number of FPS you are getting... that's to be expected if Nvidia is putting out a new GPU. People don't play games like Cyberpunk at 15fps. 

When will people who do benchmarks/reviews learn to control for testing or stop over emphasizing specific model cadence. Putting all the RTX xx70 cards against each other is one task. Getting similar FPS from them all is another task. Testing FE models exclusively to control for AIB cooler variations and custom power profiles... using the same core system to test with (and correctly matching it with a xx70 class card, not doing stupid hardware unboxed stuff like using the most powerful CPU to test a 5050). 

If you're not going to put in the effort to isolate a card to it's best case scenario and then tune settings to achieve it's best usage case, you're really just not testing anything. One needs to recognize the difference between gen 3/4/5, the game support optimizations present for a card, and also drivers. 

Using the most recent driver with a 3070 results in some pretty wonky behavior. Using an older driver, the best known driver for a 3070, would result in much better performance... and then the 5070 might not run on that same driver. Or the game chosen might not run without going past 582 drivers. 

It's never as simple as putting X Card against Y card. 

Here's a more realistic breakdown: 

The 2070 represents a small jump from the 1070, with RTX added (even though it's horrendous). It's basically a step below so many other cards one could get for either way cheaper price or much better performance. I would be better off with a 12GB 3060 or a 1080 ti than a 2070. I'm not using RTX or DLSS or any of that nonsense at this performance level, anyway. 

The 3070 represents a significant jump over the 2070, both in terms of RT and Raster performance. It's solidly 160% of a 2070, in a single generation (almost unheard of). I almost wouldn't even call the 2070 an RTX card or even in the same 'league' of cards as the 3070, 4070, and 5070. 

The 4070 is a 3070 Super at just 30% more powerful, using the same manufacturing node (basically better binned rather than a true generational leap). Buying a 4070 would be the same choice as buying a 3080 or a 6900XT. In fact, a used 3080 would be a better upgrade (depending on model, Vram amount) over any RTX 4070 (the 4070 is basically a suped up 3060 12GB). While the lower end performance metrics would line up well, at the higher end, under load, a 3080 is a better card than a 4070. Even the 10GB version. 

The 5070 is the most generationally confused out of the whole bunch. It is literally just a faster version of the 4070 GPU slapped onto the same architecture they have recycled since the RTX 3060 (192 bit 12GB Vram) but the chip is even more fasterer. Logic would tell you picking up an RTX 3060 12GB is nearly the same purchasing choice as getting a 4070 or a 5070, just spend more if you have more to spend and can find a card. 

If we are having a discussion about FPS, most gamers aren't trying to play at 1440p. Mathematically, 1080p (or another upscaled lower res) is going to perform better for a game, leading to higher FPS than 1440p. Do people play at 1440p? Sure. That's a tradeoff of  FPS for image quality.  Your entire video is trading FPS for slightly better image quality. 

I'd be interested to see what you get at 1080p across these 4 cards, I'd be interested to see the architectures tested side by side (3060 12GB vs 4070 vs 5070) and the understanding cards such as the 3070 are truly rare (both the 4070 and 5070 have smaller bus width than the 3070, despite having more Vram, going to result in worse performance at same settings). 

Finally, max FPS =/= stable FPS. Gaming is all about stable, fluid motion, clear, detailed scene. There still is no substitute for rasterized 1080p performance on maxxed settings. DLSS on a 5050 or 5060 is a crime.
